children.name: Paradisaea
children.description: The classic birds of paradise, with elongated flank plumes and iridescent plumage. Males perform elaborate courtship displays at communal leks, and the genus gave the entire family its common name after early explorers marvelled at preserved skins brought back from New Guinea.

children.name: Paradisaea apoda
children.description: The species that first introduced Europeans to the family when early traders shipped plucked skins to Europe, leading Linnaeus to name it 'apoda' (footless). Males perform inverted pendulum displays at leks, suspending themselves upside down with shimmering plumes fully fanned.

children.name: Paradisaea raggiana
children.description: Papua New Guinea's national bird, famed for its spectacular crimson flank plumes. Males gather in treetop leks where they call raucously then erupt into synchronised wing-spreading dances, each competing to impress the perching females above.

children.name: Paradisaea rudolphi
children.description: The only Paradisaea with a predominantly black-and-blue plumage, lacking the typical red flank plumes. Males perform an unusual inverted display from a horizontal perch, swaying back and forth with wings extended.

children.name: Paradisaea minor
children.description: A smaller relative of the greater bird of paradise, found in lowland forests of New Guinea. Males have elongated yellow flank plumes.

children.name: Paradisaea guilielmi
children.description: A striking species with brilliant yellow flank plumes and an emerald-green throat, found in hill forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Paradisaea decora
children.description: A spectacular bird of paradise with crimson flank plumes, found only on Fergusson and Normanby islands in the D'Entrecasteaux Archipelago.

children.name: Cicinnurus
children.description: A genus of stunning small birds of paradise noted for brilliant colours and uniquely twisted central tail wires that end in ornamental spirals. Males perform intricate micro-lek displays on fallen logs, showcasing their iridescent gorgets and fanned tail feathers.

children.name: Cicinnurus respublica
children.description: A dazzling miniature gem of the Raja Ampat islands, with a bare blue crown crossed by two black bars and a vivid crimson back. The male's tail wires curl into perfect emerald spirals that he displays on a cleared patch of forest floor.

children.name: Cicinnurus regius
children.description: The smallest and most vividly coloured bird of paradise, brilliant scarlet above with white underparts and two long tail wires ending in emerald discs. Males perform a curious bouncing dance on a vertical sapling, fanning their throat feathers into a metallic green shield.

children.name: Cicinnurus magnificus
children.description: Formerly placed in its own genus Diphyllodes, this species has a stunning yellow mantle and iridescent belly. Males clear a display arena and hang upside down from a vine, slowly opening their wings to reveal a vivid blue-green breast shield.

children.name: Lophorina
children.description: A genus best known for the superb bird of paradise's iconic 'smiley face' courtship display, achieved by raising a glossy blue-black breast shield into a perfectly circular, iridescent frill. Recent molecular work has expanded the genus to include several former Ptiloris species.

children.name: Lophorina superba
children.description: Famous for its remarkable courtship display in which the male erects a highly iridescent blue breast shield and cape to form a black oval with two bright blue crescents, resembling a broad cartoonish face. The female, drab brown, judges the male's frantic hopping from a nearby perch.

children.name: Lophorina intercedens
children.description: A recently recognized species from eastern New Guinea, with a distinctive iridescent cape used in courtship.

children.name: Lophorina niedda
children.description: A recently recognized lophorina from the Vogelkop region.

children.name: Seleucidis
children.description: A monotypic genus with the twelve-wired bird of paradise, named for the twelve stiff, wire-like shaft feathers that emerge from the flank plumes. The male's display is unique among the family, consisting of a static pose held on a vertical perch.

children.name: Seleucidis melanoleucus
children.description: A mostly black bird of paradise with a prominent yellow flank plume and twelve wire-like feathers that curve upward from the body. Unlike other birds of paradise, the male displays in silence, holding a rigid posture on a vertical perch while the female inspects him closely.

children.name: Semioptera
children.description: A monotypic genus endemic to the Maluku Islands of Indonesia, where Wallace's standardwing bird of paradise was first described by Alfred Russel Wallace. Unique among birds of paradise for having elongated white wing epaulettes rather than flank plumes.

children.name: Semioptera wallacii
children.description: Discovered by Alfred Russel Wallace on Halmahera in 1858, the male has two pairs of long white wing epaulettes that flutter like flags during an aerial display. Males gather in high canopy display trees where they leap upward, calling with nasal squeaks.

children.name: Parotia
children.description: Medium-sized birds of paradise with six wire-like head plumes and elaborate ballerina-like courtship dances on forest-floor display arenas.

children.name: Parotia sefilata
children.description: A stunning bird of paradise with six wire-like head plumes and a brilliant iridescent breast shield. Males perform an intricate ballerina dance on a cleared forest floor arena.

children.name: Parotia carolae
children.description: Named after Queen Carola of Saxony, this species has striking coppery-bronze upperparts and six wire-like head plumes.

children.name: Parotia berlepschi
children.description: A little-known parotia endemic to the Foja Mountains of New Guinea, with bronze-tinted plumage.

children.name: Parotia lawesii
children.description: A parotia found in southeastern New Guinea, with six wire-like head plumes and a spectacular courtship dance.

children.name: Parotia helenae
children.description: A parotia restricted to the mountains of southeastern Papua New Guinea, named after Princess Helena of the United Kingdom.

children.name: Ptiloris
children.description: Medium-sized birds of paradise with curved bills and highly iridescent plumage, named for their rifle-like calls. They are the most aggressive of the birds of paradise, defending feeding territories.

children.name: Ptiloris magnificus
children.description: A striking riflebird with iridescent blue-green plumage and a long curved bill, found in New Guinea and Cape York. Males display on exposed perches, raising their wings into a wide V-shape.

children.name: Ptiloris intercedens
children.description: A riflebird endemic to eastern New Guinea, with brilliant metallic green-blue plumage.

children.name: Ptiloris paradiseus
children.description: An Australian riflebird with exquisite iridescent plumage, found in the rainforests of eastern Australia.

children.name: Ptiloris victoriae
children.description: Named after Queen Victoria, this riflebird is found in the rainforests of northeastern Queensland.

children.name: Epimachus
children.description: Large birds of paradise with extremely long, decurved sickle-shaped bills and spectacular elongated tail feathers.

children.name: Epimachus fastuosus
children.description: A large, spectacular bird of paradise with a long sickle-shaped bill and an enormous fan-shaped tail, found in the montane forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Epimachus albertisi
children.description: A sicklebill with rich brown plumage and an extremely long decurved bill, found in the highlands of New Guinea.

children.name: Epimachus meyeri
children.description: A sicklebill with striking blue facial wattles and an extremely long graduated tail.

children.name: Astrapia
children.description: Long-tailed birds of paradise with iridescent plumage in metallic greens, blues, and purples.

children.name: Astrapia nigra
children.description: A stunning astrapia with iridescent green and blue plumage, found in the Arfak Mountains of New Guinea.

children.name: Astrapia splendidissima
children.description: A brilliantly coloured astrapia with metallic green and purple plumage, found in the central highlands of New Guinea.

children.name: Astrapia mayeri
children.description: The most spectacular astrapia, with two extraordinarily long white tail streamers that can reach over one metre in length.

children.name: Astrapia rothschildi
children.description: An astrapia with iridescent green and blue plumage, restricted to the Huon Peninsula of New Guinea.

children.name: Drepanornis
children.description: Medium-sized sicklebills with decurved bills and iridescent plumage.

children.name: Drepanornis bruijnii
children.description: A sicklebill with a pale, decurved bill and iridescent blue-green plumage, found in lowland forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Drepanornis albertisi
children.description: A sicklebill with dark decurved bill and iridescent plumage, restricted to the montane forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Parotia
children.description: Birds of paradise with six wire-like head plumes.

children.name: Parotia sefilata
children.description: A parotia from the Vogelkop region of New Guinea.

children.name: Parotia carolae
children.description: A parotia from the central highlands of New Guinea.

children.name: Parotia berlepschi
children.description: A parotia from the Foja Mountains.

children.name: Parotia lawesii
children.description: A parotia from southeastern New Guinea.

children.name: Parotia helenae
children.description: A parotia from southeastern Papua New Guinea.

children.name: Drepanornis
children.description: Medium-sized sicklebills from New Guinea.

children.name: Drepanornis bruijnii
children.description: A sicklebill from lowland forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Drepanornis albertisi
children.description: A sicklebill from montane forests of New Guinea.
